test_id_1.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_1.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is older and mainly points providers back to the manual and Medicaid Update articles for pharmacy claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says that for pharmacy claims, providers should refer to the eMedNY Pharmacy Billing Guidelines and the ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual: “For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy” and “Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. For dual eligible members, the policy context also states that “Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare” and that “Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]. The February 2024 Medicaid Update adds that “Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines” [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual evidence states that “the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient” and that if “the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are “required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members” and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources treat dual eligible pharmacy-related billing as involving Medicare rather than Medicaid-only billing: the policy says “Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare,” and the manual says providers must “submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members” [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources indicate that Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: the policy says “Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” while the manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment “will not exceed” the Medicaid-only amount and “no additional payment will be made” if Medicare pays more [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and stating that pharmacies may bill only for “Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” while the provider manual is broader and newer, addressing Medicare enrollment, claim submission, assignment, and payment limits for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The provider manual gives the current operational rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including that pharmacies must “submit claims and accept assignments” for Medicare-covered services and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment; the policy excerpts do not provide that level of claim-processing detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]."], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain a single, current, comprehensive definition of dual eligible pharmacy claims; they mainly reference where to find billing guidance and older immunization-related instructions. The provider manual is newer and should guide current dual eligible pharmacy claim handling, but the policy excerpts remain relevant background for the limited points they expressly state. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]"}

test_id_2.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_2.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 points users to the pharmacy manual for pharmacy claims, while older Medicaid Update language says dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare, not Medicaid billing. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source does not itself define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing rules in detail; it says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and also references the ProDUR/ECCA provider manual for claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] For dual eligible members, older Medicaid Update guidance states, \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] The newest policy source also states, \"Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines\" and Medicaid Update articles. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states, \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources direct dual eligible pharmacy-related services away from Medicaid-only billing and toward Medicare-related handling: the policy says, \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare,\" and the manual requires providers to \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources indicate that Medicaid pharmacy billing is not standalone for dual eligible situations and that providers should follow the applicable pharmacy/manual guidance rather than relying only on the update article: the policy says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy,\" and the manual sets the payment and enrollment rules for dual eligible claims. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are limited and do not provide a full dual eligible pharmacy-claim payment rule; they mainly point readers to other billing guidance and, in older language, say dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "The provider manual gives the operative current claim/payment rule for dual eligible pharmacy services: Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, and if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The policy excerpts provided are sparse and mostly cross-reference other manuals, so they do not fully define dual eligible pharmacy claim handling on their own. The provider manual is newer and should guide current practice for this issue unless the user is asking about historical policy language. "}

The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 points providers to the pharmacy manual for pharmacy claims, while older Medicaid Update language says dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt does not define dual eligible pharmacy claims in detail; it says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. For dual eligible members in the older Medicaid Update guidance, \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made\"; it also says \"the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It further states that \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources recognize that dual eligible members involve Medicare in pharmacy-related billing/payment rules: the policy says \"Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says providers must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary in this context: the policy says \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says total Medicare/Medicaid payment \"will not exceed\" the Medicaid-only amount and \"No additional payment will be made\" if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization access and directing pharmacy claims readers elsewhere: \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023], while the provider manual gives direct payment and claim-submission rules for dual eligible pharmacy services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18; 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The provider manual is more current and more specific for this issue: it requires pharmacies to \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025], whereas the policy excerpt only says dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare and does not provide the same claim-processing detail [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]."], "caveats": "The policy excerpts provided do not contain a full current definition of dual eligible pharmacy claims; the April 2023 policy chunk explicitly points to the pharmacy billing guidelines instead [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]. The provider manual is the more recent and more detailed source for current guidance on this issue [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18; 21, 22 — Oct 01, 2025]."}

test_id_5.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-02-01 is older and points providers to the pharmacy manual and Medicaid Update articles for pharmacy claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — Feb 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence does not define dual eligible pharmacy claims in detail; it says, \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy\" and also to the ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual for claims guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] The older policy update states, \"Dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and that \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,\" which is limited to immunization services rather than general pharmacy claims. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers \"are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from properly enrolled providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources tie dual eligible pharmacy-related billing to Medicare rather than treating Medicaid as the only payer: the policy says \"Dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and the manual says providers must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources indicate Medicaid payment is limited or secondary for dual eligible situations: the policy says \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,\" and the manual says \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are narrower and older, focusing on immunization services and directing billing questions elsewhere: \"Dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" and \"For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023]", "The provider manual gives more current and operational dual eligible pharmacy claim rules, including Medicare enrollment and payment limits: \"the NYS Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid\" and \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21-22, 18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain a full current definition of dual eligible pharmacy claims; they mainly point readers to other billing manuals and include an older immunization-specific rule. The provider manual is the more recent and more directly applicable source for current dual eligible pharmacy claim guidance. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — Apr 01, 2023] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21-22 — Oct 01, 2025]"}

test_id_6.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_6.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual gives the clearest current rule that pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid because \"NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and that \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance also states that \"It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says that if claim issues are resolved, \"the pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The billing guidelines add that it is \"the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025]", "similarities": ["\"NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and \"Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "\"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" and the pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid only \"after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy source states the general rule that providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, but it does not spell out the pharmacy-specific \"per the plan’s requirements\" language found in the provider manual. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "The provider manual adds operational detail that the pharmacy must determine whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurer covers the service being billed, which is not stated in the policy excerpt provided. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents."], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not include a dual-eligible-specific pharmacy claim rule beyond the general payer-of-last-resort/TPL billing requirement, so the policy side is broader than the provider manual. The newest provider manual source is later than the newest policy source, so it should guide current operational billing practice unless the user is asking about a historical period. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]"}

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid because \"NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and that \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance also states that \"It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also says that if billing a plan is difficult, the pharmacy should advise the prescriber and member of delays and may resubmit to Medicaid only after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources place primary billing responsibility on the pharmacy/provider to pursue other liable coverage before Medicaid: \"bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and \"submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other coverage: \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the provider manual requires other coverage to be billed first before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy source states the general payer-order rule in broad terms—\"bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\"—while the provider manual is more specific to pharmacy claims and says the pharmacy must submit to \"a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements\" before Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "The provider manual adds operational detail not stated in the policy excerpt: if plan billing has problems, the pharmacy should \"advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution\" and may resubmit to Medicaid only after proper TPL adjudication. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The policy excerpt does not provide that level of resubmission detail. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not include a pharmacy-manual-style operational workflow for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, so the policy side is broader than the provider manual. The question asks about dual-eligible pharmacy claims specifically, but the clearest grounded dual-eligible-specific language in the provided chunks is the provider manual’s pharmacy/TPL billing rule; the policy excerpt supports the general payer-last-resort principle rather than a detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim procedure. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]"}

test_id_8.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require the pharmacy to bill other liable coverage before Medicaid, and Medicaid remains the payer of last resort. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties liable for a claim before billing Medicaid, and that \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" because \"all other available resources\" must be used first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy guidance also states that \"it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid,\" and if issues are resolved, the pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid after proper TPL adjudication. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also states that for Medicare/Medicaid claims, \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that the combined payment, if any, \"must be accepted as payment in full.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["\"bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and \"submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage ... before submitting the claim to Medicaid\" both require other coverage to be billed first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "\"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" aligns with the manual’s rule that other coverage must be used before Medicaid is billed. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific about pharmacy workflow, stating the pharmacy has a \"corresponding responsibility\" to follow \"the plan’s requirements\" and may resubmit to Medicaid after TPL adjudication, while the policy update states the broader payer-last-resort rule without that operational detail. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "The policy source includes an older billing-direction statement that the pharmacy must bill \"the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid,\" while the provider manual excerpt provided here focuses on other coverage/TPL and Medicare/Medicaid payment limits rather than listing all possible parties. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The provided excerpts do not contain a dual-eligible-specific definition that expressly says which payer is primary in every dual-eligible pharmacy scenario; they do, however, clearly ground the general rule that other liable coverage must be billed before Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-18 — Oct 01, 2025]"}

test_id_10.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage before Medicaid, and NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 says the same general rule for pharmacy claims, so the provider manual should guide current handling of dual-eligible pharmacy claims by default. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy guidance says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid: \"NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy also states that pharmacies must bill \"to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid\" and that providers should actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states: \"Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid.\" It also says that if claim issues are resolved, \"the pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also states that for Medicare/Medicaid payment, \"The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" and that total payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only amount. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid for pharmacy claims: \"bill applicable third parties ... before billing NYS Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and \"submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage ... before submitting the claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/payer of last resort: \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] and the manual requires other coverage to be used before Medicaid and only then resubmission to Medicaid after TPL adjudication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific about process and timing, stating pharmacies have a \"corresponding responsibility\" to follow \"the plan’s requirements\" and may \"resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-17 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy update states the broader payer-of-last-resort rule without that resubmission detail [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025].", "The policy sources include broader reminders about provider responsibility to seek TPL enrollment and bill the \"appropriate party\" including \"facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy claim submission to other coverage before Medicaid and accepted payment rules for Medicare/Medicaid claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "The provided excerpts do not include a dual-eligible-specific pharmacy claim definition using that exact term; the comparison is grounded in general third-party liability / other-coverage billing rules that apply to pharmacy claims. The provider manual is newer (2025-10-01) than the newest policy update (2025-04-01), so it should receive more weight for current operational guidance. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16-18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]"}

test_id_11.query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
test_id_11.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-07-01 and earlier say Medicaid is payer of last resort and that dual-eligible members generally access Medicare first, but the provider manual adds a more specific payment rule that Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be recovered from the member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says that for duals, Medicaid is secondary to other coverage: \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] The July 2025 policy update also states that the manual update includes \"a revised end date for Medicare flexibilities and emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond these Medicare-first / payer-of-last-resort rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance is more specific on co-payment responsibility: \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Me...\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for provider manual documents beyond this payment-in-full and no-recovery rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources treat Medicare as primary and Medicaid as secondary for dual-eligible members: policy says \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort\" and that \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual says Medicare/Medicaid payment, if any, \"must be accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources indicate dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules or Medicare-based billing first: policy says the July 2025 update includes \"emphasis that duals are subject to Medicare rules\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2-3 — Jul 01, 2025], and the provider manual addresses Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance recovery limits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more explicit about co-payment responsibility because it states that \"the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full\" and that a provider \"cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts\" from the member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025], while the policy excerpts do not expressly address member co-payment recovery.", "The policy excerpts focus on billing order and payer-of-last-resort status—\"Medicaid is the payer of last resort\" and Medicare must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]—whereas the provider manual adds the specific payment consequence that no additional payment is due if Medicare payment exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "The provider manual excerpt is truncated at \"from Me...\" so the quoted sentence is incomplete in the provided chunk; however, the grounded text still clearly states the no-recovery rule for Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. The policy excerpts provided do not contain a direct co-payment responsibility rule for dual-eligible members, so the comparison is limited to the billing/payment language available in the excerpts."}

test_id_21.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare
test_id_21.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require Medicaid to be billed only after other liable coverage is billed first, but the provider manual adds a specific Medicare rule for pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "Policy documents state that NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, because Medicaid is the payer of last resort: \"NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]. The older policy reminder also says pharmacies must bill \"the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid\" and that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020].", "provider_manual_definition": "The provider manual states that pharmacies enrolled in Medicaid as billing providers \"are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program\" and that \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\"; it further says that \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage \"per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers must bill \"applicable third parties\" before Medicaid, and the manual says claims must be submitted to \"other coverage\" before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available resources: policy says \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort,\" and the manual says \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific about Medicare: it says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers \"are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program\" and that services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid \"must first be billed to Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]. The policy excerpt only says to bill applicable third parties before Medicaid and does not separately spell out this Medicare-first rule [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025].", "The policy excerpts are broader and less detailed, referring generally to \"applicable third parties\" and \"the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid,\" while the provider manual gives a pharmacy-specific billing sequence tied to Medicare participation and dual-covered services [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "The newest provider manual source is more recent than the newest policy source, so it should guide current pharmacy billing practice for this issue unless you are analyzing a historical period. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:5 — Feb 01, 2019]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual source says pharmacies enrolled as Medicaid billing providers \"are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program\" and that \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.\" It further states: \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]", "The policy excerpts frame the rule more generally as a payer-of-last-resort requirement for all third parties, whereas the provider manual frames the rule as a pharmacy billing workflow requirement tied to Medicare participation and Medicare-first billing when both programs cover the service. test_id_25.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_25.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare
test_id_25.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. It says pharmacies enrolled as billing providers must demonstrate Medicare participation and that Medicare benefits must be maximized before billing Medicaid, while the newest policy source dated 2025-04-01 says providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source states that \"NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and that \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" because \"all other available resources\" must be used first. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] The older policy reminder also says it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill \"the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid,\" but it does not separately define a Medicare-before-Medicaid rule beyond that general billing responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual source states that \"Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program\" and that \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.\" It further says that \"Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says pharmacies must submit claims to a member’s other coverage \"per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require billing other available coverage before Medicaid: the policy says providers must bill \"applicable third parties\" before NYS Medicaid, and the manual says \"Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary/last resort: the policy says \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort,\" and the manual says Medicare must be maximized before billing Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific about Medicare, stating that pharmacies enrolled as billing providers \"are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program\" and that services covered by both programs \"must first be billed to Medicare,\" while the policy excerpt only states a general third-party-before-Medicaid rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "The policy excerpt frames the rule broadly as billing \"applicable third parties\" and using \"all other available resources\" before Medicaid, whereas the manual ties the rule to pharmacy enrollment and Medicare participation requirements. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain a detailed Medicare-specific pharmacy billing rule comparable to the provider manual’s explicit instruction, so the policy side is broader and less specific on this exact question. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020]"}

test_id_31.query: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?
test_id_31.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for COB and should receive more weight by default. The policy sources dated 2025-04-01 and earlier say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other liable third parties must be billed first, but the provider manual adds a specific Workers’ Compensation rule and billing exception.", "policy_definition": "Policy documents define Coordination of Benefits (COB) as requiring that the correct party pay first: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] They state that “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment,” and that “the responsible third-party should pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] The newest policy update also says “NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid” and repeats that “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "provider_manual_definition": "The provider manual states a specific COB rule for Workers’ Compensation: “If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] It also provides an exception: “If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’ in field 309-C9.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require other available coverage to be used before NYS Medicaid/NYRx pays: “all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] and “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources treat Medicaid/NYRx as secondary or last-resort coverage: “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022] and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy documents state the general COB rule for all claims—“the correct party pays first” and “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6-7 — Oct 01, 2022]—while the provider manual gives a narrower, pharmacy-specific Workers’ Compensation rule and exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The provider manual adds an explicit billing exception not stated in the policy excerpts: if the claim is unrelated to the Workers’ Compensation case, “NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of ‘2’” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond the general last-resort/third-party-first rule, and insufficient grounded evidence to define COB comprehensively for the provider manual beyond the Workers’ Compensation pharmacy billing rule and exception. The newest provider manual source is more recent than the newest policy source, so it should guide current pharmacy COB billing questions unless the user is asking about historical policy."}

The current guidance requires using applicable third parties first; NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and for workers’ compensation cases the workers’ compensation coverage must be used before billing NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "Policy documents define COB as requiring the correct party to pay first: “Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first.” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] They state that “NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] and “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” with providers required to bill liable third parties first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7 — Apr 01, 2025].", "Both sources place primary responsibility on the liable third party before Medicaid: “the responsible third-party should pay first” [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022] and “Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific and operational for workers’ compensation claims, requiring prior use of workers’ compensation coverage and allowing NYRx to be billed as primary only when the claim is not related to the workers’ compensation case with Eligibility Clarification Code “2” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025].", "The policy updates are broader COB statements and do not provide the workers’ compensation exception or billing-code instruction found in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6 — Oct 01, 2022; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says the pharmacy must use professional judgment in a limited emergency-department scenario: “The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions.” It also says, “The pharmacy is responsible for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.” [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] Separately, policy says providers should verify directions and consult the prescriber as needed: “Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes.” [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — Sep 01, 2024] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond these responsibilities.", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual evidence states that if required member identifiers are missing, “If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these items on the prescription” when the information “Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is requesting that the prescription be filled,” or when “The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025] It also places a separate responsibility on the pharmacist to make “a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27,28 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist to use judgment/verification when prescription information is incomplete or uncertain: policy says the pharmacy is responsible for using “professional judgement” in an emergency-department scenario, and the manual requires a “good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription” when the prescriber is unknown. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — Jan 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27,28 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources require follow-up/verification rather than automatic rejection when information is missing or unclear: policy says the pharmacy must “consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes,” and the manual allows filling without entering missing member identifiers when the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4,5 — Oct 01, 2025]", "The policy excerpts focus on emergency-department prescriptions and contacting the assigned PCP the next business day, while the provider manual focuses on missing member identifiers and verifying the prescriber’s identity when the prescriber is unknown. [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0139 — Oct 01, 2025] [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid does not reimburse drugs/drug uses that include weight-loss treatment: “The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance ...” [mu_no01_jan25_pr.pdf::0047 — Jan 01, 2025]. [mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0001 — Apr 01, 2022]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual says Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the NYS List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and lists as non-reimbursable examples: “Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, ...” [Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0114 — Oct 01, 2025]. test_id_51.query: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities
test_id_51.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit; this provider manual is more up to date overall and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 is older and addresses related drug coverage/billing rules, but it does not specifically define home infusion billing in the provided excerpt.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy excerpt says NYS Medicaid FFS covers certain drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] The older policy excerpt also assigns billing responsibility to the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual excerpt states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] It further says: “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] The manual also notes that NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs may be available through both benefit types: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources tie coverage to Medicaid benefit/payment rules rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: the provider manual says there is no bundled payment for home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], and the policy excerpt says drugs are covered under NYS Medicaid FFS when they meet specified indication criteria [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].", "Both sources place billing on the provider/pharmacy side rather than the member: the policy excerpt says “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], and the provider manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is specific to home infusion and says there is no bundled payment for “drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments,” with billing through pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy excerpt provided is broader and addresses drugs administered in a practitioner’s office, not home infusion specifically [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].", "The policy excerpt explicitly assigns billing responsibility to “the pharmacy” and says it must bill “the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid” as appropriate [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], while the provider manual excerpt does not assign billing responsibility to a single entity in the quoted text; it only states the claim must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents specifically as home infusion coverage; the policy excerpts are related but not home-infusion-specific. The provider manual is the newer and more directly applicable source for the home infusion question."}

[Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — Mar 01, 2023]"], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain naloxone-specific reimbursement guidance, so the policy side is insufficient to answer the question directly. "}
